​Contextualise and analyse this extract
5




10




15




20




25




30




35		She sought to divert him, lowered her hands and said: “We’ll find a place among the Fremen?”
“The Fremen have a saying they credit to Shai-hulud, Old Father Eternity,” he said. “They say: ‘Be prepared to appreciate what you meet.’” And he thought: Yes, mother mine – among the Fremen. You'll acquire the blue eyes and a callus beside your lovely nose from the filter tube to your stillsuit and you'll bear my sister: St. Alia of the Knife.
“If you're not the Kwisatz Haderach,” Jessica said, “what –”
“You couldn't possibly know,” he said. “You won't believe it until you see it.”
And he thought: I'm a seed.
He suddenly saw how fertile was the ground into which he had fallen, and with this realization, the terrible purpose filled him, creeping through the empty place within, threatening to choke him with grief
He had seen two main branchings along the way ahead – in one he confronted an evil old Baron and said: “Hello, Grandfather.” The thought of that path and what lay along it sickened him.
The other path held long patches of gray obscurity except for peaks of violence. He had seen a warrior religion there, a fire spreading across the universe with the Atreides green and black banner waving at the head of fanatic legions drunk on spice liquor. Gurney Halleck and a few others of his father's men — a pitiful few — were among them, all marked by the hawk symbol from the shrine of his father's skull.
“I can't go that way,” he muttered. “That's what the old witches of your schools really want.”
“I don't understand you, Paul,” his mother said.
He remained silent, thinking like the seed he was, thinking with the race consciousness he had first experienced as terrible purpose. He found that he no longer could hate the Bene Gesserit or the Emperor or even the Harkonnens.
They were all caught up in the need of their race to renew its scattered inheritance, to cross and mingle and infuse their bloodlines in a great new pooling of genes. And the race knew only one sure way for this— the ancient way, the tried and certain way that rolled over everything in its path: jihad.
Surely I cannot choose that way, he thought.
But he saw again in his mind's eye the shrine of his father's skull and the violence with the green and black banner waving in its midst.
Jessica cleared her throat, worried by his silence. “Then … the Fremen will give us sanctuary?”
He looked up, staring across the green-lighted tent at the inbred, patrician lines of her face. “Yes,” he said. “That’s one of the ways.” He nodded. “Yes. They’ll call me … Muad’Dib, ‘The One Who Points the Way.’ Yes … that’s what they’ll call me.”
And he closed his eyes, thinking: Now, my father, I can mourn you. And he felt the tears coursing down his cheeks.
Extract 1 - Dune

Facts

Frank Herbert, Dune, 1965
genre: #sciencefiction

Extract

p. 170-171, very end of first chapter, "Dune"

Themes

  • ecology
  • religion
  • being chosen

Questions

  • if it’s about ecology... where is this ecology?
  • if it’s science fiction, why are there only humans?
  • why all the arab/muslim references? do we have a problem with orientalism, here?

Picture file (svg):

CheatSheet Dune

Facts

George Orwell, Nineteen Eighty-Four (1949)
genre: #dystopian

Extracts

Extract 1: Towards the end, Room 101
Towards end, when Winston is imprisoned and questioned by O’Brien.
Specifically: a photograph which doesn’t officially exist, which has officially never exist-ed is suddenly shown to Winston. He has seen it before, by accident.

  • Analyse O’Brien and Winston, starting from this passage
  • How is their relationship?
  • What is Winston’s relationship to Julia, and how does it develop?

Extract 2:
Beginning of the novel (p. 26-27)
intense worldbuilding
characterisation of Winston

many things seem "wrong":

  • clock strikes 14
  • 25cent-piece, etc.
    Big Brother
    slogans

Themes: Worldbuilding

  • Describe the characters! (protagonist, antagonist)
  • Why is Winston imprisoned?
  • Describe the world in 1984
  • Who has power in the state, Oceania?
  • How and why does the Party establish and maintain power?
    • Surveillance
      • What is the outlook on technology Orwell casts here?
    • What is doublethink?
    • Two Minutes Hate
    • What does the party do to the language, and why?
    • Do you think this is a valuable means of controlling people?
    • Changing history: is this legitimate? Would it work? Don’t we do it by in-terpreting history anew for each generation?
    • Creating an enemy, maintaining external threat
    • Why: only interested in power, nothing else (explicitly stated)
  • Who is Big Brother?
  • What is the background for Orwell to write this novel?
  • What’s the relevance of the novel today? (surveillance, totalitarian states?)
  • What do you make of the TV show of the same name: Isn’t that a little bit scary?

Characters

Dystopian fiction in general

  • What is the background for Orwell to write this novel?
  • Compared to The Road: nature of “the threat” / child of its time?
  • What’s the relevance of the novel today? (surveillance, totalitarian states?)
  • What do you make of the TV show of the same name: Isn’t that a little bit scary?
CheatSheet 1984

Facts

J.R.R. Tokien, The Hobbit, 1937
genre: #fantasy

characters

  • characterise Bilbo
  • Thorin
  • Gandalf
  • other dwarves: Balin, Dwalin, Bifur, Bofur, Bombur, Dori, Nori, Ori, Fili, Kili, Oin, Gloin
  • Bert, Tom, William (trolls)
  • Lord of the Eagles
  • Beorn (skin-changer)
  • Elvenking
  • Master of Lake-Town (ruler of Esgaroth)

themes

  • bravery
  • breaking out of cosiness and the known in order to gain something
  • quest
    • to find gold hoard
    • to kill a dragon
    • restore justice
    • help friends
    • find adventure
  • property: stealing and stealing back? stealing to survive (ring)?
  • animals with human attributes; helpers
  • riddles, and:
  • the power of language (maybe more in The Lord of the Rings trilogy)
  • songs and poems
  • old history and stories

structure and narrative

  • what is the function of foreshadowing?
  • what role does the narrator assume?
  • what function do the songs and poems have in the narrative?

fantasy

  • why fantasy? why in 1937, why today? general function?

The Hobbit as a travelogue

CheatSheet the Hobbit

To create transitions between Dune, 1984, and The Hobbit, consider the following thematic connections:
Power and Control: In Dune, Paul Atreides grapples with the immense power of foresight and the burden of leadership, similar to the totalitarian control in 1984. Both explore how power can shape societies and individual destinies, though Dune focuses on prophecy and free will, while 1984 examines surveillance and oppression.
World-Building and Societal Structures: Frank Herbert's Dune and J.R.R. Tolkien's The Hobbit are renowned for their intricate world-building. Both authors create detailed universes with unique social systems—Dune with its feudal class structure and The Hobbit with its diverse races and cultures. This shared focus on world-building invites readers to explore complex societies and their histories.
The Role of Prophecy and Fate: In Dune, Paul Atreides' foresight and the tension between fate and free will are central themes. Similarly, in The Hobbit, prophecies and the concept of destiny play significant roles, such as Bilbo's unexpected journey and the foretold return of the King under the Mountain. Both narratives explore how characters navigate their destinies and the impact of prophecy on their lives.
These connections can help bridge discussions between the three works, highlighting their shared exploration of power, societal structures, and destiny.
